questions.text: I feel that the moral pull of another person's suffering is among the most trustworthy signals I can follow.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the consideration that most decisively separates what I should do from what is merely convenient.
I believe that pouring myself out for others is the most fulfilling way to spend my energy.
I feel that the process of planning itself helps me understand what I'm trying to achieve.
I believe that life rewards those who are ready to seize unexpected opportunities.
I find that I don't need much recovery time after emotionally charged situations.
I carry the emotional tone of a dream well into my waking day.
I feel that my job is to point the way, even if others resist.
I think of myself as someone who facilitates outcomes.
I am careful to manage my energy across the day so I don't burn out before evening.
I believe that the ability to evaluate competing claims of need without emotional favoritism is essential for fair resource distribution.
I notice that my appetite and energy fluctuate dramatically with my emotional state.
I find it hard to enjoy something if I know someone else had to go without.
I feel that my personal goals are just as important as anyone else's needs.
I detect the subtle disappointment in someone's voice when they expected a different response.
I find that I do not instinctively sense who the social connectors are in a group.
I sense the difference between a comfortable lull in conversation and an awkward one.
I am the person who usually calls the meeting or sets the agenda.
